---
title: "Meta-Analysis"
---

<span class="section-badge advanced">Advanced Topics</span>

Meta-analysis is a statistical method for **synthesising effect sizes across multiple studies**. Rather than relying on a single study, a meta-analysis pools estimates to arrive at a more precise, generalisable conclusion about an effect.

---

## When to use meta-analysis

- You have collected effect sizes (or sufficient statistics) from multiple independent studies on the same research question
- Your thesis includes a systematic review with a quantitative synthesis component
- You want to quantify **heterogeneity** — do effect sizes vary across studies, and if so, why?

---

## Key concepts

### Effect size metrics

Choose the effect size metric appropriate for your data:

| Data type | Effect size metric |
|---|---|
| Two group means (continuous DV) | Cohen's *d* / Hedges' *g* |
| Correlational data | Fisher's *z*-transformed *r* |
| Frequencies / proportions | Log odds ratio (log OR) |
| Means across time / conditions | Standardised mean change |

### Fixed-effects vs. random-effects models

| Model | Assumption | Use when |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-effects** | All studies estimate the same true effect | Exact replications — rarely appropriate |
| **Random-effects** | Studies estimate different true effects drawn from a distribution | Different studies, different populations — almost always preferred |

In a **random-effects model**, the variance of the distribution of true effects (τ²) is estimated. A larger τ² indicates more heterogeneity.

### Heterogeneity

| Statistic | What it tells you |
|---|---|
| **Q** | Total variability in effect sizes; significant *Q* indicates heterogeneity beyond sampling error |
| **I²** | Percentage of total variability due to true heterogeneity (not sampling error). I² = 25% (low), 50% (moderate), 75% (high) as rough guides |
| **τ** (tau) | The standard deviation of the distribution of true effects — in the original effect size metric |

---

## How to do it in JASP

Install the Meta-Analysis module: click the blue **+** icon in the top-right of the JASP window to open the module list, then enable **Meta-Analysis**.

The JASP Meta-Analysis module is a graphical interface to the R package **`metafor`** (Viechtbauer, 2010), with additional post-processing on top. Behind the scenes, every analysis maps to a `metafor` call, which means you get the full state-of-the-art set of methods — but through a point-and-click interface (Bartoš, Wagenmakers & Viechtbauer, 2025).

The typical workflow has three stages: (1) compute effect sizes from the raw study results, (2) inspect the data with a funnel plot, (3) fit a meta-analytic model and visualise it with a forest plot. Each stage is its own analysis under the *Meta-Analysis* menu.

### Stage 1 — Compute effect sizes from raw data

Open **Meta-Analysis → Effect Size Computation**. In the *Design*, *Measurement*, and *Effect size* dropdowns, pick the configuration matching your data (e.g. *Independent groups*, *Binary*, *RR* for risk ratios; or *Independent groups*, *Continuous*, *SMD* for standardised mean differences). Then drag the relevant outcome and group-size columns into the boxes. The output panel reports how many effect sizes were computed successfully.

![The *Effect Size Computation* dialog on the BCG-vaccine dataset: thirteen trials reporting positive (`tpos`) and negative (`tneg`) tuberculosis cases in vaccinated and control groups. JASP computes the log risk ratio and standard error for each study and stores them as new columns in the data.](../assets/images/blog/meta-analysis/bartos-003-000.png){fig-align="center" width=620}

This stage calls `metafor::escalc()` behind the scenes — every effect-size choice that `escalc` supports (RR, OR, RD, SMD, ROM, ZCOR, PR, IR, …) is available in the dropdown. Chain multiple computations with the green **+** button if you have several outcomes.

### Stage 2 — Inspect the data with a funnel plot

Open **Meta-Analysis → Funnel Plot**. Drag the computed effect-size and standard-error columns into the input panel. JASP draws each study as a point in effect-size × standard-error space, with the *funnel* representing the sampling distribution under the chosen null. Asymmetry around the centre line is the visual hint of publication bias (formal tests live in the *Publication Bias / Sensitivity Analyses* section — see below).

### Stage 3 — Fit a meta-analytic model and draw a forest plot

Open **Meta-Analysis → Classical Meta-Analysis**. Drag *Effect Size* and *Effect Size Standard Error* into the data inputs. The defaults follow `metafor` recommendations: **REML** for the random-effects variance, and the **Knapp–Hartung** small-sample adjustment for the fixed-effects test (Knapp & Hartung, 2003). Tick *Forest Plot* to visualise; the figure renders alongside the pooled estimate, heterogeneity statistics, and the underlying `metafor` R code for reproducibility.

![The *Classical Meta-Analysis* output for the BCG vaccine data. The model summary (top right) gives a pooled risk ratio of 0.49 with heterogeneity *Q*(12) = 152.23, *p* < .001. The forest plot (bottom right) shows each of the 13 trials, the diamond at the bottom is the pooled effect, and JASP exposes the `metafor` code at the bottom for export to R if needed.](../assets/images/blog/meta-analysis/bartos-005-002.png){fig-align="center" width=720}

### Beyond the basics — meta-regression and moderators

Drag one or more variables into the *Predictors* box of the Classical Meta-Analysis dialog to fit a **meta-regression** — the same model, but with covariates explaining between-study heterogeneity. Tick *Estimated Marginal Means and Contrasts* to get marginal means at specific predictor levels, and tick *Bubble Plot* to visualise the meta-regression slope:

![A *meta-regression* on the writing-to-learn dataset (Example 2 of Bartoš et al., 2025) with two predictors. The output gives an omnibus test for each predictor (top right), individual coefficients, marginal-mean tables across levels of `feedback`, and a bubble plot of effect size against `length` with study weights encoded by bubble size.](../assets/images/blog/meta-analysis/bartos-006-004.png){fig-align="center" width=720}

### Advanced models (multilevel, multivariate, cluster-robust SEs)

For nested data (effect sizes within studies within research groups), open the *Random Effects / Model Components* section in the Classical Meta-Analysis dialog and add a **Nested (multilevel)** component, specifying the grouping variables at each level. For dependent effect sizes (e.g. several outcomes per study), the *Effect Size Variance-Covariance Matrix* analysis lets you specify the dependency structure that goes into the model. Both surfaces also support **cluster-robust standard errors** with one tickbox.

These advanced models put JASP roughly on par with `metafor` itself for what's expressible through the GUI — multilevel, multivariate, location–scale meta-regression, and small-sample corrections are all accessible without writing a single line of R.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Meta-Analysis
Short walkthrough recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html):

- [▶ Effect Size Computation](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html#effect-size-computation) — computing *d*, *r*, or log OR from raw statistics
- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html#main-analysis) — random-effects model, forest plot, heterogeneity statistics

For the full guided walkthrough on real datasets (BCG vaccines, writing-to-learn interventions, recidivism), see **Bartoš, F., Wagenmakers, E.-J., & Viechtbauer, W. (2025). Meta-Analysis with JASP, Part I: Classical Approaches.** arXiv:2509.09845. Annotated JASP files: [osf.io/mb56t](https://osf.io/mb56t/).
:::

---

## Reading a forest plot

A forest plot shows:

- Each study as a horizontal line: the **point estimate** (square) and its **confidence interval** (the line)
- The size of the square is proportional to the study's **weight** (usually inverse variance)
- The **diamond** at the bottom shows the pooled effect and its CI
- The dashed vertical line is the pooled effect; the solid vertical line at 0 (or 1 for ORs) is the null

---

## Publication bias

Studies with null results are less likely to be published, which can inflate meta-analytic estimates. Assess with:

- **Funnel plot**: plot effect size vs. standard error — asymmetry suggests publication bias
- **Egger's test**: a regression test for funnel plot asymmetry
- **Trim-and-fill method**: imputes "missing" studies to correct for asymmetry

All of these are available in JASP's meta-analysis module.

---

## Moderator analysis

If I² is high (> 50%), explore **moderators** — study-level variables (e.g., participant age, intervention type, publication year) that might explain heterogeneity. In JASP, add these as covariates in a **meta-regression**.

---

## Interpreting the output

::: {.callout-tip}
## APA reporting example
A random-effects meta-analysis of *k* = 18 studies (total *N* = 2,341) yielded a pooled effect of *d* = 0.43, 95% CI [0.28, 0.58], *z* = 5.60, *p* < .001. Heterogeneity was moderate, *Q*(17) = 34.2, *p* = .007, I² = 50%. There was no evidence of publication bias based on Egger's test (*t*(16) = 1.21, *p* = .24).
:::

---

## Further reading

- **Borenstein, M., Hedges, L. V., Higgins, J. P. T., & Rothstein, H. R. (2009).** *Introduction to meta-analysis*. Wiley. — The standard text.
- **[Cochrane Handbook](https://training.cochrane.org/handbook)** — comprehensive guide for systematic reviews in health research.
- **Viechtbauer, W. (2010).** Conducting meta-analyses in R with the `metafor` package. *Journal of Statistical Software*, 36(3), 1–48. — The R package JASP's Meta-Analysis module builds on.
- **Bartoš, F., Wagenmakers, E.-J., & Viechtbauer, W. (2025).** Meta-analysis with JASP, Part I: Classical approaches. arXiv:2509.09845. — End-to-end tutorial with three worked examples (BCG vaccines, writing-to-learn, recidivism) covering effect-size computation through multilevel and multivariate models. Annotated JASP files at [osf.io/mb56t](https://osf.io/mb56t/).

---

## Try it yourself

Meta-analysis is not part of the DSUJ Labcoat Leni / Smart Alex companion set, so there is no pre-packaged worked-solution dataset on discoverjasp. The examples below use the `metafor` package's bundled datasets, which JASP can import via the CSV/RData routes.

### Exercise — Pool the BCG vaccine trials

The classic meta-analysis teaching dataset is **`dat.bcg`** from the R package `metafor` — 13 trials of the BCG vaccine against tuberculosis with binary outcomes. Export it from R with `metafor::dat.bcg` and `write.csv(metafor::dat.bcg, "bcg.csv")`, then import into JASP. Fit a **random-effects** meta-analysis on the log risk ratios, request a **forest plot**, and inspect the heterogeneity statistics (Q and I²).

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked walkthrough
- **In R / metafor**: the `dat.bcg` example is documented in [the metafor analysis examples](https://www.metafor-project.org/doku.php/analyses) (the random-effects model with log RR is the standard demonstration).
- **In JASP**: the [JASP Video Library — Meta-Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/Meta-Analysis.html) shows the equivalent workflow end-to-end.
:::

### Exercise — Moderator analysis on the same data

Using the same dataset, add **ablat** (absolute latitude of the trial) as a moderator. Does latitude explain a significant portion of the between-study heterogeneity? Reproduce the classic result that vaccine effectiveness increases with latitude.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked walkthrough
- **`metafor`** users can fit this as `rma(yi, vi, mods = ~ ablat, data = dat.bcg, method = "REML")` and inspect the moderator's *p*-value, R², and residual heterogeneity. JASP's Meta-Analysis module exposes the same model via the *Moderators* input.
:::
